home.heroSubtitle: The community layer for AI agent skills
home.heroDescription: Discover, create, fork, and share reusable capabilities for Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and 18+ AI agents.
home.heroQualityNote: Every listing is reviewed by the Skill Advisor for quality, security, and compatibility.
home.cliHint: Search, browse, and install skills from your CLI
home.highestRated: Highest rated by the Skill Advisor
home.pluginsDescription: Skills with plugin support for full features in Claude Code
home.findSkills: Find skills for your specific use case
home.skillsExplainer1: SKILL.md files package domain expertise — best practices, coding conventions, debugging workflows, and framework-specific knowledge — into reusable capabilities that any AI agent can leverage.
home.skillsExplainer2: Think of them as expert playbooks: Install one, and your AI agent instantly gains specialized knowledge for React, Rails, AWS, or whatever domain you need.
home.installStep1: Find a skill above or search the marketplace
home.installStep2: Click the install command to copy it
home.installStep3: Paste into your terminal — the skill installs instantly
home.createOwnDesc: It's just a SKILL.md file in your repo. Run npx mdskills create to generate one, then submit it to the marketplace.
home.shareSkills: Share your skills, MCP servers, workflows, and rulesets with the ecosystem.

skills.title: Agent Skills
skills.description: SKILL.md files package domain expertise into something any AI agent can use. They define when to activate, what practices to follow, and how to handle edge cases — so your agent works like an expert from the first prompt.

specsSkillMd.metaDescription: SKILL.md is the open standard for creating modular, reusable capabilities for AI coding agents. Learn the format, frontmatter fields, and how 27+ agents discover and use skills.
specsSkillMd.metaOgTitle: SKILL.md: The Agent Skills Format | mdskills.ai
specsSkillMd.metaOgDescription: The open standard for creating modular, reusable capabilities for AI coding agents. Supported by Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, and 27+ agents.
specsSkillMd.title: SKILL.md — The Agent Skills Format
specsSkillMd.intro: SKILL.md is an open standard for packaging reusable capabilities that AI coding agents can discover and activate on demand. Created by Anthropic and standardized at
specsSkillMd.introSuffix: , the format is supported by 27+ AI agents including Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Gemini CLI, and VS Code.
specsSkillMd.problemText: Without skills, you repeat the same complex prompts every time you want an agent to do something specific — generate a PDF, build a design system, write Playwright tests. SKILL.md packages those instructions once so any compatible agent can pick them up automatically. Skills are loaded on demand, so they don't bloat your agent's context window.
specsSkillMd.howItWorksText: A skill is a folder containing a SKILL.md file with YAML frontmatter and markdown instructions. At minimum, a skill needs a name and description.
specsSkillMd.fieldNameDesc: Unique identifier. Max 64 chars, lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens.
specsSkillMd.fieldDescriptionDesc: What the skill does and when to activate it. Max 1024 chars.
specsSkillMd.fieldLicenseDesc: SPDX license identifier or reference to a license file.
specsSkillMd.fieldCompatibilityDesc: List of compatible agents and environment requirements.
specsSkillMd.fieldAllowedToolsDesc: Space-delimited list of pre-approved tools for the agent.
specsSkillMd.progressiveText: Skills use a three-phase loading model to keep context windows efficient:
specsSkillMd.phase1: Discovery — Agent reads only the name and description (~100 tokens)
specsSkillMd.phase2: Activation — When a task matches, the full SKILL.md is loaded (<5000 tokens)
specsSkillMd.phase3: Execution — Agent follows instructions, loading referenced files as needed
specsSkillMd.supportsText: The SKILL.md format is supported by Claude Code, Cursor, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, VS Code, GitHub Copilot, Amp, Roo Code, Goose, Windsurf, Continue, and many more. The full list of compatible agents is maintained at
specsSkillMd.relatedAgentsMd: AGENTS.md — project-level context (loaded every prompt, not on demand)
specsSkillMd.relatedMcp: MCP — for connecting agents to external tools and services
specsSkillMd.officialSpecText: The full SKILL.md specification is maintained at agentskills.io.

docsHub.metaTitle: Docs: AI Agent Skills, Plugins, MCP Servers & Rules
docsHub.metaDescription: Everything you need to work with AI agent skills, plugins, MCP servers, and rules. Getting started guides, best practices, specs, and compatible agents.
docsHub.metaOgDescription: Everything you need to work with AI agent skills, plugins, MCP servers, and rules. Getting started, best practices, and specs.
docsHub.title: Documentation
docsHub.intro: mdskills.ai is a directory of skills, plugins, MCP servers, and rules for AI coding agents. Whether you're installing your first skill or building your own, this is where to start.
docsHub.gettingStartedDesc: Install your first skill in under a minute. Works with Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, and 20+ other agents.
docsHub.step1Desc: Explore skills, plugins, MCP servers, or rules.
docsHub.step3Desc: Your agent discovers the skill automatically. Just ask it to do something the skill teaches.
docsHub.whatIsIntro: AI agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex are powerful out of the box — but they don't know your specific workflows, conventions, or domain. The AI agent ecosystem has converged on a set of open formats to bridge this gap:
docsHub.skillsLayer: Skills (SKILL.md) — modular capabilities loaded on demand. "How to generate release notes in our format."
docsHub.pluginsLayer: Plugins — full-featured extensions bundling skills, commands, hooks, and agents into one installable package.
docsHub.mcpLayer: MCP Servers — external tools that let agents call APIs, query databases, and access services via the Model Context Protocol.
docsHub.rulesLayer: Rules (CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, AGENTS.md) — project-level constraints. "Never commit .env files. Always run tests before pushing."
docsHub.whatIsOutro: mdskills.ai indexes all of these so you can find, compare, and install them in one place.
docsHub.rulesLayerDesc: Project-wide constraints that apply to every task. Rules tell agents what to always (or never) do — coding conventions, safety constraints, testing requirements.
docsHub.skillsPluginsDesc: Modular capabilities loaded on demand. Skills teach agents how to do specific tasks. Plugins bundle multiple skills with commands, hooks, and agents.
docsHub.toolsServersDesc: External capabilities that let agents take action — call APIs, query databases, search the web. MCP is the standard for connecting agents to tools.
docsHub.guideSkillsDesc: The SKILL.md format, progressive disclosure, and how agents discover skills.
docsHub.guideCreateDesc: Step-by-step guide to writing, validating, and publishing your first SKILL.md.
docsHub.guideBestDesc: Practical tips for writing skills that agents use well.
docsHub.guideVsDesc: When to write a skill and when to build an MCP server.
docsHub.guideInstallDesc: Where to put SKILL.md files for every major agent.
docsHub.guideExamplesDesc: Annotated walkthroughs of four skill patterns that work.
docsHub.guideMcpDesc: The Model Context Protocol — connecting agents to external tools.
docsHub.guideAgentsDesc: 27+ AI agents that support SKILL.md, MCP, and more.
docsHub.specsIntro: Technical reference for the open formats powering the AI agent ecosystem.

docsInstall.metaDescription: Step-by-step instructions for installing SKILL.md files on Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code, Codex, and other AI agents. Covers project skills, personal skills, and CLI tools.
docsInstall.metaOgTitle: How to Install Agent Skills | mdskills.ai
docsInstall.metaOgDescription: Step-by-step instructions for installing SKILL.md files on Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code, Codex, and other AI agents.
docsInstall.title: How to Install Agent Skills
docsInstall.intro: Skills are just folders with a SKILL.md file. Installing one means putting that folder in the right place for your agent. Here's where that place is for each tool.
docsInstall.universalText: Every agent reads skills from a specific directory. There are two scopes:
docsInstall.projectSkillsDesc: Live inside your repo. Committed to version control. Available to everyone working on that project.
docsInstall.personalSkillsDesc: Live in your home directory. Available across all your projects. Only you can use them.
docsInstall.manualText: The simplest approach — no CLI tools needed:
docsInstall.manualStep1: Find a skill on mdskills.ai and click the Download button on its detail page.
docsInstall.manualStep2: Create the skill folder in your project. For example, for Claude Code:
docsInstall.manualStep4: Restart your agent (or start a new session). The skill is now available.
docsInstall.folderNameNote: The folder name becomes the skill name. A folder called review-pr becomes the /review-pr command in agents that support slash commands.
docsInstall.whereToSaveText: See the agent-by-agent guide below for the exact directory path for your specific tool. The short version: most agents use .'{agent}'/skills/'{name}'/SKILL.md in your project root.
docsInstall.cliText: The fastest way to install any skill from a GitHub repo:
docsInstall.cliAutoDetect: CLI tools detect your installed agents and place skills in the right directories automatically. But you don't need a CLI — copying the folder manually works just as well.
docsInstall.claudeCodeNotes1: Claude Code has the deepest skills support. Skills show up as slash commands — a skill named review-pr becomes /review-pr. Claude also loads skills automatically when your request matches a skill's description.
docsInstall.claudeCodeNotes2: If you have existing files in .claude/commands/, those still work. Skills are the newer format and support extra features like bundled scripts and frontmatter configuration.
docsInstall.claudeCodeNotes3: Claude Code also supports plugins, which can bundle multiple skills together with MCP servers and other extensions. Install plugins via /plugins in the CLI.
docsInstall.cursorNotes1: Cursor launched its marketplace in February 2026 with support for skills as part of its plugin system. Skills are one of five plugin primitives alongside MCP servers, subagents, hooks, and rules.
docsInstall.cursorNotes2: You can also place skills in .agents/skills/ — this path is recognized by multiple agents and is a good choice if your team uses different tools.
docsInstall.vscodeNotes1: VS Code supports agent skills through GitHub Copilot. Skills go in .github/skills/ for project skills, which means they're automatically tracked in version control alongside your code.
docsInstall.vscodeNotes2: VS Code also recognizes .claude/skills/ and .agents/skills/. You can configure additional search paths with the chat.agentSkillsLocations setting.
docsInstall.vscodeNotes3: Type / in the Copilot chat to see available skills and invoke them directly.
docsInstall.codexNotes: Codex uses the .agents/skills/ directory by default. This is the vendor-neutral path that multiple agents recognize, making it a safe bet if you're publishing skills that need to work across different tools.
docsInstall.geminiNotes: Gemini CLI also reads from .agents/skills/ as a fallback. If you're targeting Gemini CLI specifically, use the .gemini/skills/ path. For cross-agent compatibility, use .agents/skills/.
docsInstall.crossAgentText: If you're publishing a skill and want it to work everywhere, use the .agents/skills/ path. Most agents check this directory as a fallback. For project-specific skills where your team all uses the same tool, use the vendor-specific path.
docsInstall.crossAgentTip: Tip: You can have the same skill in multiple directories. Some teams symlink their skills across paths so they work regardless of which agent someone uses. A simple ln -s does the job.
docsInstall.verifyText: After installing a skill, check that your agent can see it:
docsInstall.verifyClaudeCode: Claude Code: Ask "What skills are available?" or type / to see skills in the autocomplete menu.
docsInstall.verifyVscode: VS Code: Type / in the Copilot chat pane.
docsInstall.verifyAny: Any agent: Ask "Do you have a skill for [task]?" and see if it picks up the description.
docsInstall.verifyTroubleshoot: If the skill doesn't show up, double-check the folder structure. The directory name must contain a SKILL.md file (case matters).

docsAdvisor.metaTitle: Skill Advisor: How We Review Skills
docsAdvisor.metaDescription: How the mdskills.ai Skill Advisor evaluates skills, plugins, and MCP servers for capabilities, quality, and security.
docsAdvisor.metaOgTitle: Skill Advisor: How We Review Skills | mdskills.ai
docsAdvisor.metaOgDescription: Our AI-powered review methodology for evaluating agent skills on capabilities, quality, and security.
docsAdvisor.title: Skill Advisor
docsAdvisor.intro: Every skill, plugin, and MCP server on mdskills.ai is reviewed by the Skill Advisor — an AI-powered evaluation that scores listings on capabilities, quality, and security. The goal is to help you quickly assess whether a skill is well-built, safe to use, and worth installing.
docsAdvisor.evaluateText: The Skill Advisor analyzes the SKILL.md content (and README when available) against three dimensions:
docsAdvisor.capabilitiesText: What does this skill actually enable an agent to do? Is it useful and well-scoped, or shallow and trivial? Are the instructions specific enough that an agent could execute them reliably without guessing?
docsAdvisor.qualityText: Is the SKILL.md well-structured? Does it have clear trigger conditions, step-by-step instructions, examples, and edge case handling? Does it use progressive disclosure? Would an agent or human understand exactly what this does out of the box?
docsAdvisor.securityText: Are the declared permissions appropriate for what the skill actually does? Are there unvalidated shell commands, unconstrained file writes, or credential handling concerns? Could a malicious input trick an agent into running dangerous commands through this skill?
docsAdvisor.scoringText: Each skill receives a score from 1 to 10, along with specific strengths and weaknesses. The score reflects overall quality across all three dimensions:
docsAdvisor.scoreHigh: Strong — actionable, well-structured, and immediately usable by an agent
docsAdvisor.scoreMid: Decent — functional but could improve in specificity, examples, or permissions
docsAdvisor.scoreLow: Weak — missing actionable instructions or not usable by an agent as-is
docsAdvisor.scoringNote: Scores of 9 or 10 are rare and reserved for skills that excel in all three dimensions. A score of 7 is good. Security concerns — especially undeclared permissions or prompt injection risk — significantly impact the score.
docsAdvisor.flagPermissions: Permission mismatches — a skill that uses shell commands but doesn't declare shell execution
docsAdvisor.flagOverscoped: Over-scoped permissions — requesting filesystem write, shell, and network when only read is needed
docsAdvisor.flagMissing: Missing instructions — a skill that describes what it does but not how to do it
docsAdvisor.flagTrigger: No trigger conditions — no clear "when to use this skill" section
docsAdvisor.flagInjection: Prompt injection surface — instructions that could be exploited by malicious file content
docsAdvisor.flagCredentials: Credential handling — secrets stored in plaintext or passed unsafely
docsAdvisor.howGeneratedText1: Reviews are generated by Claude (Anthropic's AI) using a structured evaluation prompt. The reviewer analyzes the skill's SKILL.md content, README, and declared permissions. Reviews are generated when a skill is first imported and can be regenerated when the skill content is updated.
docsAdvisor.howGeneratedText2: The Skill Advisor is not a popularity metric. It doesn't consider GitHub stars, install counts, or community sentiment. It evaluates the skill file itself — its structure, clarity, security posture, and usefulness to an AI agent.
docsAdvisor.limitation1: Reviews are AI-generated and may occasionally miss nuances or produce false positives
docsAdvisor.limitation2: The review evaluates the skill file, not the underlying tool or service it wraps
docsAdvisor.limitation3: Security analysis is based on content inspection, not runtime testing
docsAdvisor.limitation4: Scores may vary slightly if a review is regenerated
docsAdvisor.improveScoreText: Read the SKILL.md Best Practices guide — especially the security section on permissions, shell safety, and credential handling.
docsAdvisor.feedbackText: If you think a review is inaccurate or unfair, let us know. We're continuously improving the evaluation criteria to be more helpful and precise.
